{{Short description|Academic Award}}
The '''Exeter Prize''' is an economics prize of the [[University of Exeter Business School]], which has been awarded since 2012. The Exeter Prize is awarded to the best paper published in the previous calendar year in a peer-reviewed journal in the fields of [[Experimental Economics]], [[Decision Theory]] and [[Behavioural Economics]].

== Winners ==
* 2025: Tomas Jagelka for Jagelka, T. (2024) “Are Economists’ Preferences Psychologists’ Personality Traits? A Structural Approach”, ''Journal of Political Economy'', '''132'''(3).<ref>https://groups.google.com/g/esa-announce/c/5sujtj5eRDw?pli=1</ref> 
* 2024: Erin Bronchetti, Judd Kessler, Ellen Magenheim, Dmitry Taubinsky and Eric Zwick for Bronchetti, E. T., Kessler, J. B., Magenheim, E. B., Taubinsky, D., & Zwick, E. (2023). "Is attention produced optimally? theory and evidence from experiments with bandwidth enhancements." ''Econometrica'', '''91'''(2): 669–707.
* 2023: Kirby Nielsen and John Rehbeck  for Nielsen, K., & Rehbeck, J. (2022). "When Choices are Mistakes." ''American Economic Review''. '''112''' (7): 2237–2268. 
* 2022: Sandro Ambuehl, [[Douglas Bernheim]] and [[Axel Ockenfels]]  for Ambuehl, S., Bernheim, B. D., & Ockenfels, A. (2021). "What Motivates Paternalism? An Experimental Study." ''American Economic Review''. '''111''' (3): 787-830. 
* 2021: Ryan Opera for Oprea, Ryan (2020). "What Makes a Rule Complex?". ''American Economic Review''. '''110''' (12): 3913–3951. [[Doi (identifier)|doi]]:10.1257/aer.20191717  <ref>{{Cite journal |last=Oprea |first=Ryan |date=December 2020 |title=What Makes a Rule Complex? |url=https://www.aeaweb.org/articles?id=10.1257/aer.20191717 |journal=American Economic Review |language=en |volume=110 |issue=12 |pages=3913–3951 |doi=10.1257/aer.20191717 |issn=0002-8282|url-access=subscription }}</ref>
* 2020: J. Aislinn Bohren, Alex Imas and Michael Rosenberg  for {{cite journal | title= The Dynamics of Discrimination: Theory and Evidence| journal=[[The American Economic Review|American Economic Review]] | volume=109 | number=10 |year=2019 | pages=3395–3436 | doi=10.1257/aer.20171829| last1=Bohren|first1=J. Aislinn| last2=Imas| first2=Alex| last3=Rosenberg| first3=Michael| url=https://www.aeaweb.org/articles?id=10.1257/aer.20171829}}
* 2019: Samuel M. Hartzmark and Kelly Shue for {{cite journal | title=A Tough Act to Follow: Contrast Effects in Financial Markets| journal=[[The Journal of Finance|Journal of Finance]] |volume=73 |number=4| year=2018| pages=1567–1613|doi=10.1111/jofi.12685| last1=Hartzmark | first1=Samuel| last2=Shue| first2=Kelly | url=http://www.nber.org/papers/w23883.pdf }}
* 2018: Shengwu Li for {{cite journal | title=Obviously Strategy-Proof Mechanisms| journal=[[The American Economic Review|American Economic Review]] |volume=107 |number=11| year=2017| pages=3257–87 |doi=10.1257/aer.20160425| last1=Li | first1=Shengwu | url=https://mpra.ub.uni-muenchen.de/78930/1/MPRA_paper_78930.pdf }}
* 2017: Vojtěch Bartoš, Michal Bauer, Julie Chytilová and Filip Matějka for {{cite journal |title=Attention Discrimination: Theory and Field Experiments with Monitoring Information Acquisition |journal=[[The American Economic Review|American Economic Review]] |volume=106 |number=6 |year=2016 |pages=1437–75 |doi=10.1257/aer.20140571 |last1=Bartoš |first1=Vojtěch |last2=Bauer |first2=Michal |last3=Chytilová |first3=Julie |last4=Matějka |first4=Filip |citeseerx=10.1.1.1032.2725 }}
* 2016: [[David Budescu]] and Eva Chen for {{cite journal |title=Identifying Expertise to Extract the Wisdom of the Crowds |journal=[[Management Science (journal)|Management Science]] |volume=61 |issue=2 |year=2015 |pages=267–280 |doi=10.1287/mnsc.2014.1909 |last1=Budescu |first1=David V. |last2=Chen |first2=Eva }}
* 2015: [[Gary Charness]], Francesco Feri, Miguel Melendez and Matthias Sutter for {{cite journal |title=Experimental games on networks: underpinnings of behavior and equilibrium selection |journal=[[Econometrica]] |volume=82|number=5|year=2014|pages=615–1670|doi=10.3982/ECTA11781|citeseerx=10.1.1.638.1113 }} <ref>[https://www.royalholloway.ac.uk/economics/news/newsarticles/2014-15/congrulationsproffrancescoferi.aspx "Congratulations Prof. Francesco Feri"], ''[[Royal Holloway, University of London]]'', 21 July 2015.</ref><ref>[http://www.broomcenter.ucsb.edu/node/677 "Broom associate Gary Charness, was awarded the 2015 Exeter Prize for Research in Experimental Economics, Decision Theory and Behavioral Economics"], ''[[University of California, Santa Barbara]]''.</ref><ref>[http://sutter.wiso.uni-koeln.de/en/news/single-view/?tx_news_pi1%5Bnews%5D=3614&cHash=b65ef7dfb2c868b153e4b1f1183db041 "Professor Matthias Sutter has won the 2015 Exeter Prize for Research in Experimental Economics, Decision Theory and Behavioral Economics"]{{Dead link|date=August 2019 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }}, ''[[University of Cologne]]''.</ref><ref>[http://www.andalusianstories.com/shiny-people/in-science/miguel-angel-melendez-experimental-economics/ "Miguel Ángel Meléndez, experiments to understand how markets work"], ''Andalusian Stories'', 14 June 2016. Retrieved on 14 September 2016.</ref>
* 2014: Tomasz Strzalecki for {{cite journal |title=Temporal Resolution of Uncertainty and Recursive Models of Ambiguity Aversion |journal=[[Econometrica]]|volume=81|number=3 |year=2013|pages=1039–1074|doi=10.3982/ECTA9619  |url=http://nrs.harvard.edu/urn-3:HUL.InstRepos:12967691}} <ref>[http://economics.harvard.edu/news/professor-tomasz-strzalecki-wins-2014-exeter-prize "Professor Tomasz Strzalecki Wins 2014 Exeter Prize"], ''[[Harvard University]]'', 8 April 2015.</ref>
* 2013: Daniel Friedman and Ryan Oprea for {{cite journal |title=A Continuous Dilemma |journal=[[American Economic Review]] |volume=102|number=1|year=2012|pages=337–63| doi=10.1257/aer.102.1.337|last1=Friedman |first1=Daniel |last2=Oprea |first2=Ryan }} <ref>[http://economics.ucsc.edu/news-events/profiles/friedman-exeter-prize.html "Daniel Friedman and Ryan Opera Win 2013 Exeter Prize"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140418064633/http://economics.ucsc.edu/news-events/profiles/friedman-exeter-prize.html |date=2014-04-18 }}, ''[[University of California, Santa Cruz]]'', 9 January 2014.</ref>
* 2012: Michel Regenwetter, Jason Dana and Clintin P. Davis-Stober for {{cite journal |title=Transitivity of Preferences |journal=[[Psychological Review]] |year=2011 | doi = 10.1037/a0021150 |pmid=21244185 |volume=118 |issue=1 |pages=42–56 | last1 = Regenwetter | first1 = Michel | last2 = Dana | first2 = Jason | last3 = Davis-Stober | first3 = Clintin P.|s2cid=36833965 }} <ref>[https://internal.psychology.illinois.edu/events/announcements.php?MoreInfo=260 "Michel Regenwetter, Jason Dana and Clintin P. Davis-Stober receive The Exeter Prize for Research in Experimental Economics, Decision Theory and Behavioral Economics"], ''[[University of Illinois at Urbana–Champaign]]'', 20 August 2012.</ref>
